```{=latex}
\appendix
```

# Feature Inventory

The tables below catalogue every feature type implemented in the feature
registry. The subset used in the main
state-space experiment is marked in the **In model** column: ✓ indicates
all variants are used, while a specific parameter value indicates only that
variant is used.
Feature names match the keys produced by the feature pipeline at runtime.

All features used in the main HFT experiment are z-score normalized with causal
running statistics that are updated chronologically and reset at detected
session boundaries, with two exceptions noted below the tables: the cyclical
time encodings, which are inherently bounded in $[-1, 1]$, and the runtime
position feature, which is already bounded in $[-1, 1]$ by the action space
definition.

## LOB Microstructure Features

**Notation.**
$P^{bid}_i$, $P^{ask}_i$: bid/ask price at book level $i$;
$V^{bid}_i$, $V^{ask}_i$: resting volume at level $i$;
$C^{bid}_i$, $C^{ask}_i$: order count at level $i$;
$M_t = \tfrac{1}{2}(P^{bid}_0+P^{ask}_0)$: mid-price;
$S_t = P^{ask}_0-P^{bid}_0$: spread;
$\Delta V^{bid}_t = V^{bid}_{0,t}-V^{bid}_{0,t-1}$, $\Delta V^{ask}_t = V^{ask}_{0,t}-V^{ask}_{0,t-1}$: tick-to-tick volume change;
$W$: rolling window in events;
$\Sigma_W\,x_t = \sum_{\tau=t-W+1}^{t} x_\tau$: rolling sum;
$\bar{x}_W$: rolling mean over $W$ events;
$V^B_W$, $V^S_W$: rolling buy/sell volume over $W$ events;
$L$: odd-lot size threshold (100 shares);
$Q$: notional or size threshold;
$h_t$: hour of day (0–23);
$\Delta t_{\text{ns}}$: inter-event time in nanoseconds;
$\mathbf{1}[\cdot]$: indicator function;
$\hat{\rho}(\cdot,\text{lag}=1)$: sample autocorrelation at lag 1.

```{=latex}
\begin{landscape}
\footnotesize
\begin{longtable}{p{0.18\linewidth} p{0.09\linewidth} p{0.34\linewidth} p{0.09\linewidth} >{\centering\arraybackslash}p{0.07\linewidth} p{0.15\linewidth}}
\caption{Full LOB feature registry with formulas and citations.\label{tbl-lob-features}} \\
\toprule
Feature & Group & Formula & Param & In model & Citation \\
\midrule
\endfirsthead
\multicolumn{6}{l}{\footnotesize\textit{Table \ref{tbl-lob-features} continued.}} \\
\toprule
Feature & Group & Formula & Param & In model & Citation \\
\midrule
\endhead
\bottomrule
\endlastfoot
\texttt{hft\_book\_pressure} & Imbalance & $(V^{bid}_i - V^{ask}_i)/(V^{bid}_i + V^{ask}_i)$ & $i \in \{0,1,2\}$ & $\checkmark$ & Cao et al.\ (2004); Biais et al.\ (1995) \\
\texttt{hft\_order\_book\_imbalance} & Imbalance & $\dfrac{\sum_{i<N}(V^{bid}_i-V^{ask}_i)}{\sum_{i<N}(V^{bid}_i+V^{ask}_i)}$ & $N=3$ & $\checkmark$ & Cont et al.\ (2014) \\
\texttt{hft\_order\_count\_imbalance} & Imbalance & $(C^{bid}_i-C^{ask}_i)/(C^{bid}_i+C^{ask}_i)$ & $i=0$ & level 0 & Biais et al.\ (1995) \\
\texttt{hft\_microprice} & Fair value & $(V^{ask}_0 P^{bid}_0 + V^{bid}_0 P^{ask}_0)/(V^{bid}_0+V^{ask}_0)$ & --- & $\checkmark$ & Stoikov (2018) \\
\texttt{hft\_microprice\_divergence} & Fair value & Microprice $- M_t$ & --- & $\checkmark$ & Stoikov (2018) \\
\texttt{hft\_vwmp\_skew} & Fair value & $(\bar{P}^{vw}_N - M_t)/S_t$;\enspace $\bar{P}^{vw}_N = \dfrac{\sum_{i<N}(P^{bid}_i V^{bid}_i + P^{ask}_i V^{ask}_i)}{\sum_{i<N}(V^{bid}_i+V^{ask}_i)}$ & $N=3$ & $\checkmark$ & Stoikov (2018) \\
\texttt{hft\_price\_vamp} & Fair value & Avg.\ execution price for fixed-notional order walked through $N$ levels & $Q\in\{1k,5k\}$ & & --- \\
\texttt{hft\_depth\_ratio} & Depth & $(V^{bid}_0+V^{ask}_0)\,/\,\sum_{i=1}^{N}(V^{bid}_i+V^{ask}_i)$ & $N=4$ & & Bouchaud et al.\ (2002) \\
\texttt{hft\_spread\_bps} & Spread/cost & $(P^{ask}_0-P^{bid}_0)/M_t \times 10{,}000$ & --- & $\checkmark$ & Kyle (1985); Glosten \& Milgrom (1985) \\
\texttt{hft\_spread\_ratio} & Spread/cost & $\mathrm{SpreadBps}_t\,/\,\bar{\mathrm{SpreadBps}}_W$ & $W=50$ & $W=50$ & Easley et al.\ (2012) \\
\texttt{hft\_bid\_convexity} & Spread/cost & $(P^{bid}_0-P^{bid}_1)-(P^{bid}_1-P^{bid}_2)$ & bid & $\checkmark$ & Bouchaud et al.\ (2002) \\
\texttt{hft\_ask\_convexity} & Spread/cost & $(P^{ask}_2-P^{ask}_1)-(P^{ask}_1-P^{ask}_0)$ & ask & $\checkmark$ & Bouchaud et al.\ (2002) \\
\texttt{hft\_bid\_slope} & Spread/cost & $(P^{bid}_0-P^{bid}_4)\,/\,\sum_{i<5}V^{bid}_i$ & 5 levels & $\checkmark$ & Bouchaud et al.\ (2002); Kyle (1985) \\
\texttt{hft\_ask\_slope} & Spread/cost & $(P^{ask}_4-P^{ask}_0)\,/\,\sum_{i<5}V^{ask}_i$ & 5 levels & $\checkmark$ & Bouchaud et al.\ (2002); Kyle (1985) \\
\texttt{hft\_ofi} & Order flow & $\Delta V^{bid}_t\cdot\mathbf{1}[P^{bid}_{0,t}\geq P^{bid}_{0,t-1}] - \Delta V^{ask}_t\cdot\mathbf{1}[P^{ask}_{0,t}\leq P^{ask}_{0,t-1}]$ & --- & $\checkmark$ & Cont et al.\ (2014) \\
\texttt{hft\_ofi\_rolling} & Order flow & $\Sigma_W\,\mathrm{OFI}_t$ & $W=50$ & $W=50$ & Cont et al.\ (2014) \\
\texttt{hft\_ofi\_multilevel} & Order flow & $\sum_{i<N}\mathrm{OFI}^{(i)}_t$ (event classification at each level $i$) & $N\in\{3,5\}$ & & Cont et al.\ (2014) \\
\texttt{hft\_bid\_queue\_depletion} & Order flow & $\max(V^{bid}_{0,t-1}-V^{bid}_{0,t},\,0)\,/\,V^{bid}_{0,t-1}$ & bid & $\checkmark$ & Foucault et al.\ (2005) \\
\texttt{hft\_ask\_queue\_depletion} & Order flow & $\max(V^{ask}_{0,t-1}-V^{ask}_{0,t},\,0)\,/\,V^{ask}_{0,t-1}$ & ask & $\checkmark$ & Foucault et al.\ (2005) \\
\texttt{hft\_signed\_trade\_flow} & Order flow & $\Sigma_W\,\mathrm{size}_\tau\cdot(\mathbf{1}[\mathrm{side}_\tau=B]-\mathbf{1}[\mathrm{side}_\tau=A])$ & $W=50$ & $W=50$ & Lee \& Ready (1991) \\
\texttt{hft\_odd\_lot\_trade\_ratio} & Order flow & $\Sigma_W\,\mathbf{1}[\mathrm{trade}\wedge\mathrm{size}<L]\,/\,\Sigma_W\,\mathbf{1}[\mathrm{trade}]$ & $W=200$ & $\checkmark$ & Boehmer et al.\ (2021) \\
\texttt{hft\_odd\_lot\_imbalance} & Order flow & $(V^{B,\mathrm{odd}}_W-V^{S,\mathrm{odd}}_W)/(V^{B,\mathrm{odd}}_W+V^{S,\mathrm{odd}}_W)$; size $<L$ & $W=200$ & $\checkmark$ & Boehmer et al.\ (2021) \\
\texttt{hft\_vpin} & Order flow & $|V^B_W-V^S_W|\,/\,(V^B_W+V^S_W)$ & $W\in\{100,500\}$ & & Easley et al.\ (2012) \\
\texttt{hft\_cancel\_to\_trade} & Order flow & $\Sigma_W\,\mathbf{1}[\mathrm{action}=C]\,/\,\Sigma_W\,\mathbf{1}[\mathrm{action}=T]$ & $W\in\{200,1000\}$ & & Foucault et al.\ (2005) \\
\texttt{hft\_trade\_arrival\_rate} & Order flow & $\Sigma_W\,\mathbf{1}[\mathrm{action}=T]$ & $W\in\{100,500\}$ & & Engle (2000) \\
\texttt{hft\_large\_trade\_ratio} & Order flow & $\Sigma_W\,\mathbf{1}[\mathrm{trade}\wedge\mathrm{size}\geq Q]\,/\,\Sigma_W\,\mathbf{1}[\mathrm{trade}]$ & $W=200$ & & --- \\
\texttt{hft\_ofi\_autocorr} & Order flow & $\hat{\rho}(\mathrm{OFI}_{t-W:t},\,\mathrm{lag}=1)$ & $W\in\{50,200\}$ & & Cont et al.\ (2014) \\
\texttt{hft\_inter\_event\_time} & Regime/temp. & $\log(1+\Delta t_{\mathrm{ns}})$ & --- & $\checkmark$ & Engle (2000) \\
\texttt{hft\_mid\_price\_acceleration} & Regime/temp. & $M_t-2M_{t-1}+M_{t-2}$ & --- & $\checkmark$ & Abergel et al.\ (2016) \\
\texttt{hft\_hour\_sin} & Regime/temp. & $\sin(2\pi h_t/24)$ & --- & $\checkmark$ & Wood et al.\ (1985) \\
\texttt{hft\_hour\_cos} & Regime/temp. & $\cos(2\pi h_t/24)$ & --- & $\checkmark$ & Wood et al.\ (1985) \\
\end{longtable}
\end{landscape}
```

---

# TD3 Hyperparameters

The table below lists all hyperparameters for the main state-space
experiment.

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | | |
| Actor hidden dims | [128, 64] | ReLU activations |
| Critic hidden dims | [128, 64] | ReLU activations |
| **Optimization** | | |
| Actor learning rate | 0.0001 | Adam |
| Critic learning rate | 0.0001 | Adam |
| Actor weight decay | 0.000002 | L2 regularization |
| Critic weight decay | 0.000002 | |
| Loss function | Smooth L1 (Huber) | |
| **TD3 mechanisms** | | |
| Discount factor $\gamma$ | 0.995 | |
| Target update $\tau$ | 0.001 | Soft update |
| Policy delay | 2 | Critic updates per actor update |
| Target policy noise $\sigma$ | 0.2 | Added to target action |
| Noise clip | 0.3 | |
| Exploration noise std | 0.1 | During training only |
| **Training** | | |
| Total steps | 2,000,000 | |
| Initial random steps | 1,000 | Warm-up before learning |
| Frames per batch | 200 | |
| Optimisation steps per batch | 5 | |
| Mini-batch size | 128 | |
| Replay buffer size | 100,000 | |
| **Data** | | |
| Training rows | 15,000 | Chronological split |
| Reward type | Differential Sharpe | $\eta = 0.01$ |
| Transaction fee | 0.000 | Baseline setting |
| Random seed | 42 | |

: TD3 hyperparameters for the main state-space experiment. {#tbl-td3-hyperparams longtable=true tbl-colwidths="[36,18,46]"}

---

# Main Experiment Specification

The table below condenses the main experiment into a self-contained
research specification. It records the empirical choices needed to reproduce
the experiment without exposing implementation-specific file names.

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| Asset and data | AAPL, MSFT, TSLA, META, AMZN, and AVGO; ten-level limit order book observations during regular U.S. trading hours |
| Training size | 15,000 chronologically ordered observations |
| Feature set | Causal LOB microstructure feature set: imbalance, fair-value, spread and cost, order-flow, and regime features |
| Environment backend | Continuous single-asset trading environment |
| Action | Target portfolio exposure $a_t \in [-1, 1]$ |
| Reward | Differential Sharpe Ratio with $\eta = 0.01$ |
| Transaction fee | 0.000 in the baseline setting |
| Algorithm | TD3 |
| Network widths | Actor and critic hidden layers [128, 64] |
| Learning rates | Actor 0.0001; critic 0.0001 |
| Discount factor | $\gamma = 0.995$ |
| Target update | $\tau = 0.001$ |
| Policy delay | 2 critic updates per actor update |
| Target policy noise | $\sigma = 0.2$, clipped at $\pm 0.3$ |
| Exploration noise | $\sigma = 0.1$ during training |
| Total training steps | 2,000,000 |
| Mini-batch size | 128 |
| Replay buffer size | 100,000 |
| Loss function | Smooth L1 (Huber) |
| Random seed | 42 |

: Main TD3 experiment specification. {#tbl-main-experiment-spec longtable=true tbl-colwidths="[28,72]"}

---

# TD3 Training Workflow Diagram

The diagram below summarizes the structured training workflow described in
Chapter 5.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {
  'theme': 'base',
  'themeVariables': {
    'fontSize': '11px',
    'primaryColor': '#f5f4ff',
    'primaryBorderColor': '#a78bfa',
    'lineColor': '#6b7280'
  },
  'flowchart': {
    'nodeSpacing': 18,
    'rankSpacing': 20,
    'padding': 4,
    'useMaxWidth': false
  }
}}%%
flowchart TD
    A[Experiment specification + feature set] --> B[Pre-run validation]
    B --> C[build_prepared_dataset]
    C --> D[PreparedDataset<br/>train / val / test + metadata]
    D --> E[build_training_bundle]
    E --> F[TrainingBundle<br/>train env + trainer + callback]
    F --> G[Train TD3]
    G --> H[Checkpoint + MLflow log]
    H --> I[evaluate_all_splits]
    I --> J[Resolve primary split<br/>test -> val -> train]
    J --> K[Primary-split explainability]
    K --> L[Metrics + robustness outputs]
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

---

# TD3 Update Mechanics Diagram

The diagram below summarizes the core TD3 update mechanics used in the thesis
implementation, abstracting away from project-specific plumbing.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {
  'theme': 'base',
  'themeVariables': {
    'fontSize': '10px',
    'primaryColor': '#f5f4ff',
    'primaryBorderColor': '#a78bfa',
    'lineColor': '#6b7280'
  },
  'flowchart': {
    'nodeSpacing': 18,
    'rankSpacing': 18,
    'padding': 4,
    'useMaxWidth': false
  }
}}%%
{{< include ./diagrams/td3_update.mmd >}}
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

---

# TD3 Network Architecture

The diagram below shows how the actor, twin critics, and replay buffer interact
in the TD3 architecture used here.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%| fig-width: 3.5
%%| fig-height: 3.5
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '9px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 12, 'rankSpacing': 16, 'padding': 3, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
graph TD
    S[State s] --> A1[Actor mu_theta]
    A1 --> A2[Action a]
    A2 --> N1[Gaussian Noise added]
    N1 --> A3[Noisy Action]
    S --> C1[Critic Q_phi1]
    S --> C2[Critic Q_phi2]
    A2 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    C1 --> MIN[min]
    C2 --> MIN
    MIN --> T1[Target Value]
    RB[Replay Buffer<br/>100k transitions] --> C1
    RB --> C2
    RB --> A1
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

---

# Data Preparation Pipeline

The diagram below shows the feature pipeline design that ensures no information
from the validation or test splits leaks into the fitted normalization
parameters.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%| fig-width: 3.5
%%| fig-height: 6.8
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '8px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 10, 'rankSpacing': 14, 'padding': 3, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
{{< include ./diagrams/data_pipeline.mmd >}}
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

---

# Offline Feature Selection Pipeline

The feature selection procedure described in Section 4.2 operates in two stages:
theory constrains the candidate pool to mechanisms grounded in market
microstructure literature; IC/ICIR ranking selects among the available
implementations of those mechanisms and removes redundancy via a conditional IC
filter. This appendix describes the operational pipeline that implements Stage 2
of that procedure. It takes a theory-defined candidate pool as input, scores each
feature against a forward return proxy, and stores the selected feature
specification used by the training pipeline.

## Feature Group Registry

Features are organized into named groups that mirror the state-space design in
Section 4.2. Each group corresponds to a distinct microstructure mechanism:

| Group | Description | Features |
|---|---|:---:|
| `imbalance` | Order book imbalance signals | 5 |
| `fair_value` | Fair value and microprice estimates | 3 |
| `spread` | Spread and cost features | 6 |
| `flow` | Order flow and trade flow features | 7 |
| `regime` | Regime and temporal features | 4 |

: Feature groups used by the state-space design. The imbalance, fair value, spread, and flow groups correspond to the four signal categories in Section 4.2; the regime group provides temporal context. {#tbl-feature-groups}

The group registry lists the full specification (name, type, parameters,
normalization flag, domain tag) for each feature within each group. Groups can
be composed by name, and individual features can be excluded, without editing
the feature source code.

## Selection Procedure

The offline selection procedure takes a candidate pool of features, scores
each one against a proxy target, and applies a greedy redundancy filter to
produce a reduced set. The procedure is run once before training; the resulting
feature list is stored as the selected feature specification used by the
training pipeline. This ensures that feature selection is not repeated on every
training run and that the reduced specification is reproducible.

**Step 1: Theory-constrained candidate pool construction.** The user specifies
which feature groups to include. Each group corresponds to a distinct
microstructure mechanism (see Section 4.2); only groups with a theoretical prior
are eligible for inclusion. The feature resolver expands group names into a flat
list of feature specifications. Individual features can be excluded at this
stage, but no feature from outside the theory-defined groups can be added. This
constraint is enforced by the group registry structure, not by post-hoc
filtering.

**Step 2: Proxy target.** Each feature is scored against a forward log-return
target at horizon $h$:
$$y_t = \log \frac{p_{t+h}}{p_t}$$ {#eq-app-forward-return}
This proxy is a cheap, model-free measure of directional predictability; it is
not the reward function used during training, but it provides a consistent
benchmark for comparing feature informativeness.

**Step 3: Scoring.** Each feature is evaluated using the Information Coefficient
(IC) framework standard in quantitative factor research. The IC of feature $f_i$
at time $t$ is its Spearman rank correlation with the forward return target:
$$\text{IC}_i(t) = \rho_{\text{Sp}}\!\left(f_i(t),\, y(t)\right)$$ {#eq-app-ic}
computed over rolling windows on the validation split. The primary ranking
criterion is the Information Coefficient Information Ratio (ICIR):
$$\text{ICIR}_i = \frac{\overline{\text{IC}}_i}{\sigma(\text{IC}_i)}$$ {#eq-app-icir}
where $\overline{\text{IC}}_i$ is the mean IC and $\sigma(\text{IC}_i)$ is its
standard deviation. ICIR captures both signal strength (the numerator) and
signal consistency across time (the denominator); a feature with a high mean IC
but erratic sign is penalized relative to one with a modest but stable IC.
Features with $|\text{ICIR}_i| < 0.02$ are removed before the redundancy step.
The t-statistic $t_i = \overline{\text{IC}}_i / (\sigma(\text{IC}_i) / \sqrt{n})$
is reported alongside ICIR as a measure of statistical significance.

**Step 4: Redundancy filter.** Features are ranked by ICIR. Starting from the
highest-ranked, each candidate's residual signal is evaluated: the linear
component attributable to already-selected features is regressed out of the
candidate, and its ICIR is recomputed on the residuals. A candidate is included
in the selected set only if it retains meaningful incremental predictability
after conditioning on the features already chosen. This conditional IC approach
is a principled alternative to pairwise correlation thresholding: two features
can be highly correlated yet both informative if each predicts a distinct
component of the forward return.

**Step 5: Output.** The selected feature specifications are stored as the
feature set used by the training pipeline. The training run reads this fixed
feature set at runtime; no selection logic runs during training.

The diagram below summarizes the offline feature selection pipeline.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%| fig-width: 1.9
%%| fig-height: 3.75
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '8px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 12, 'rankSpacing': 16, 'padding': 4, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
flowchart TD
    GRP["Feature-group catalogue<br/>(5 named groups)"]
    RES["Feature resolver<br/>expand group names"]
    POOL["Candidate Pool<br/>(25 features)"]

    GRP --> RES --> POOL

    POOL --> FIT["Fit Pipeline<br/>on Training Split"]
    FIT --> TRANS["Transform<br/>Train + Validation"]
    TRANS --> SCORE["Score Features<br/>ICIR_i = mean(IC_i) / std(IC_i)"]
    SCORE --> RANK["Rank by ICIR<br/>drop if |ICIR| < 0.02"]
    RANK --> FILTER["Conditional IC Filter<br/>residualize on selected set"]
    FILTER --> SEL["Selected Features<br/>(top_k = 12)"]
    SEL --> SPEC["Selected feature specification"]
    SPEC --> TRAIN["Training Pipeline"]

    style GRP fill:#e1f5fe
    style SEL fill:#e8f5e9
    style SPEC fill:#e8f5e9
    style TRAIN fill:#fff3e0
    style SCORE fill:#fff3e0
    style FILTER fill:#fce4ec
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

The offline nature of this pipeline is deliberate: the proxy target and scoring
metrics are not the reward function that the agent optimizes during training.
Running selection as a separate step ensures that the agent's training loop
remains uncontaminated by any pre-training statistical analysis that could
introduce look-ahead bias or circular reasoning.
